# The Origin of the Northern Lights

In the time before memory, when the Sky was young and empty, there lived a weaver named Astrid who possessed an extraordinary gift. Her tapestries shimmered with colors no eye had ever seen, woven from threads she spun from her own dreams.

One winter, Astrid's village faced endless darkness. The sun had grown weak, barely rising above the horizon. The people grew cold and afraid. Astrid climbed the highest mountain, carrying her finest threads – silver from hope, green from spring memories, purple from twilight, and pink from her daughter's laughter.

At the summit, she began to weave, hanging her tapestry across the empty northern sky. But the wind was fierce, tearing her work apart. Each thread scattered across the heavens, dancing and swirling in the frozen air.

The Sky, moved by Astrid's sacrifice, breathed life into the scattered threads. They began to dance eternally, painting waves of color across the darkness. Astrid never returned from the mountain, but her gift remained.

Now, when the northern lights appear, people remember the weaver who gave her dreams to illuminate the darkness. And on the clearest nights, some say they can see her silhouette among the colors, still weaving hope into the sky.
myths.Agent_2:
# The Tears of the Moon Goddess

Long ago, when the world was young, the Moon Goddess Selara fell deeply in love with a mortal fisherman named Kael. Each night, she would descend from the heavens to walk along the shore with him, their love forbidden by the ancient laws of the cosmos.

The Sun God, her brother, discovered their secret meetings and grew furious. As punishment, he demanded Kael choose: remain mortal and never see Selara again, or become immortal but be transformed into pure water, formless and scattered.

Kael chose transformation, hoping that even as water, he could still touch his beloved. The Sun God kept his word, turning Kael into the vast ocean that encircles the world.

Heartbroken, Selara wept for seven days and seven nights. Her tears fell into the waters where Kael had become, and they were tears of both sorrow and joy—salt mixed with love. To this day, the ocean tastes of salt from Selara's tears, and each night she rises to gaze upon the waters, her reflection dancing across the waves in an eternal embrace with her beloved Kael.

This is why the tides follow the moon, and why the ocean will forever taste of tears.

Now Orion's stories shine as stars, and Aurora still delays each dawn, reading his celestial poetry. This is why dawn comes slower in winter—she has more verses to read in the longer darkness.

Lyra herself became the first star of evening, the shepherd's star, watching over her scattered gift. The shadow-creatures fled, unable to survive in a world where light hides in so many small places. And so we learned that sometimes the greatest gifts are those broken apart and shared with all.

When winter came and refused to leave, freezing the world in eternal ice, the people begged Theron for help. He carried his symphony to the frozen wasteland and released it into the bitter wind, hoping its warmth would melt the ice. Instead, the cold shattered the ribbons into countless fragments.
